era_context: The early 14th century in Eurasia was marked by the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire into competing khanates, the rise of the Ming Dynasty's precursors in China, the consolidation of Ottoman power in Anatolia, and the late medieval period in Europe characterised by feudal instability, dynastic conflicts, and the early stages of the Black Death's approach. Islamic civilisation remained vibrant across the Middle East and North Africa, while Indian sultanates competed for regional dominance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)
Central Asian nomadic populations (sparse written documentation)

events.title: Siege of Kallimere (Kallimere/Kallimeria) — Ottoman expansion in Bithynia
events.description: Ottoman forces under Osman I or his successor Orhan I besieged and captured the Byzantine fortress of Kallimere (also known as Kallimeria or Kalamaria), a strategic stronghold in northwestern Anatolia. This conquest was part of the systematic Ottoman expansion into former Byzantine territories during the early 14th century. The fall of Kallimere represented a significant step in Ottoman consolidation of Bithynia and demonstrated the military superiority of Ottoman forces over declining Byzantine garrisons. The capture contributed to the eventual Ottoman domination of the region and the erosion of Byzantine territorial control.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Aşıkpaşazade, Neşri); later Byzantine sources
Archaeological evidence of Ottoman fortification layers

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this region is well-attested, but precise dating of individual fortress captures is often uncertain. Kallimere's fall is traditionally assigned to this period, but sources vary by 1–3 years.
cross_references: Relates to broader Ottoman expansion documented in 1320s–1340s

events.title: Famine and plague conditions in China — Yuan Dynasty crisis
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under the Mongol Khanate experienced severe famine, disease, and social unrest in 1331 and surrounding years. Contemporary Chinese sources record widespread crop failures, flooding, and the outbreak of epidemic disease (possibly plague or cholera) across northern and central China. These catastrophes killed hundreds of thousands and contributed to the destabilisation of Yuan rule. The famines and epidemics are documented in Yuan official records and later Ming histories, and they coincided with the early stages of what would become the Black Death pandemic. These disasters fuelled peasant rebellions and accelerated the decline of Mongol authority in China.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Emperor Wenzong of Yuan, r. 1328–1332)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled by Ming historians
Contemporary Chinese local records and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Famine and disease in Yuan China in 1331 are well-documented in multiple sources. The dating is secure within ±1 year.
cross_references: Part of broader Black Death pandemic origins (1340s–1350s)

events.title: Battle of Halidon Hill — English victory in Scottish Wars
events.region: Halidon Hill, Scottish Borders (modern-day Scotland/England border)
events.description: English forces under Edward III defeated a Scottish army commanded by Archibald Douglas at Halidon Hill on 19 July 1331. The English employed massed longbowmen in a defensive formation, inflicting heavy casualties on the Scottish cavalry and infantry. The victory secured English control over the border region and supported the claim of Edward Balliol (a rival claimant to the Scottish throne) against David II. Halidon Hill demonstrated the tactical superiority of English longbow formations and became a model for English military doctrine in the Hundred Years' War. The battle was a significant moment in the Anglo-Scottish conflict and reinforced English dominance in the north.

sources.name: Froissart, Chronicles (compiled later but based on contemporary accounts)

events.certainty_note: The date, location, and outcome of Halidon Hill are well-documented in contemporary English records and near-contemporary chronicles. The dating is secure.
cross_references: Part of the Wars of Scottish Independence (1296–1357)
Precursor to Hundred Years' War tactics (1337–1453)

events.title: Reign of Toghon Temür (Wenzong) — Yuan Dynasty political instability
events.description: Toghon Temür (Emperor Wenzong) ruled the Yuan Dynasty from 1328 to 1332, a period marked by factional conflict, administrative corruption, and the early stages of the dynasty's decline. In 1331, his reign was characterised by struggles between Mongol and Han Chinese bureaucratic factions, financial instability, and the aforementioned famines and epidemics. Wenzong attempted administrative reforms but lacked the authority to enforce them effectively. His reign exemplified the broader crisis of Yuan governance in the early 14th century, as Mongol rule over China became increasingly contested and unstable.
key_figures: Bayan of the Merkits (powerful Mongol official)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Contemporary Yuan official records and edicts

events.certainty_note: Wenzong's reign dates and major political events are well-documented in Yuan records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Halidon Hill has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. While traditionally presented as a decisive turning point in Anglo-Scottish relations, recent historians note that the battle's long-term strategic impact was limited; Scottish independence was ultimately secured despite this English victory, and Edward Balliol's claim to the Scottish throne proved unsustainable. The battle is better understood as one episode in a prolonged conflict rather than a watershed moment. Additionally, the precise casualty figures reported in contemporary chronicles are likely exaggerated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1331 is uneven. European sources (particularly English chronicles) are relatively rich for military and political events. Chinese sources are abundant but often compiled centuries later (Yuan Shi was written by Ming historians). Islamic and Ottoman sources are sparser and often later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Oceania, and much of Central Asia are essentially undocumented for this specific year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Kallimere's capture is part of systematic Ottoman territorial consolidation in Anatolia
Yuan famines and epidemics of 1331 are among the earliest documented outbreaks of plague that would become the Black Death
Halidon Hill demonstrated longbow tactics that would define English strategy in the Hundred Years' War
Wenzong's weak rule contributed to Yuan decline and eventual Ming conquest
